title: 1.3.1 - Anatomy of a piece of paper | Drawing Machines 101

segments.text: Well, hi. It's good to see you again.
I'm just making my way into the studio
for what's going to be a very short
quick video, which is basically about
the anatomy of a piece of paper.
It's very windy and cold out here, so
let's get in.
So, a lot of us are used to dealing with
screen resolutions and pixels and
canvases and and digital stuff, things
like that. It's really dark, isn't it?
Hold on.
All right, then. That's a little bit
better. So, we're going to be artists
who are dealing with sheets of paper
that have physical dimensions. I know
it's obvious, but let's just get this
measured. This is 420 ms by 297 mm.
That's an A3 sheet of paper. We got this
huge sheet here, which is 760 mm by
what's that? 568
mm. You have all these different sizes.
We're mainly going to be using A4. You
might be using other sizes, legal, it it
doesn't really matter, but I'm going to
be using this. It's 210 mm by 297 mm.
And if I'm doing a pen plot, I want to
kind of avoid the edges. I want some
margins. So, let's say we use a 25 mm by
25 mm margin. So, 25 here, 25 here and
here. I actually normally prefer a
little bit more down at the bottom. So,
25 25 30 maybe. Regardless, that means
that with a little bit of maths, this is
160 mm of drawing area.
And down here is going to be 247
mm of drawing area.
So, that's what we get to draw in. And
now we can kind of talk about resolution
a little bit. So, here I have a pen.
Hey, cute. It says, "Hug me." And it's a
0.5 mm nib, which means that if we
wanted to draw and fill in this area all
the way across here, 160 mm, 0.5 nib, I
would need to draw 320 lines
across this page to fill this in. And
that's kind of what I mean by
resolution. Maybe this is a better way
of explaining it. When we're thinking
about designing stuff for pen plotting,
we're thinking about a combination
between nib size and paper size. That's
kind of our resolution. So, if we have
this is a failed plot, but we've got
this postcard size thing here. But if we
use the same pen on this like tiny piece
of paper like this, then it'll just look
like a a solid black mess. But if we use
this same pen or a 0.5 mm on this sheet
of paper here, then you'll just have
very few lines that are very thin and
very far apart. So, all of this is to
say that we're going to be thinking in
terms of millimeters and dimensions.
We're not going to be thinking about
pixels and screen resolution. We're
going to be thinking of millime, 10 mm,
20 mm. How long is this line? How big is
this piece of paper? How thick is the
nib of a pen? That's all this video is
all about. So, I think we've got it
pretty much covered. There is one more
thing. Let me show you. Don't worry,
you're not going to get tested on this.
I just thought I'd want to mention it.
Here's our pen plotter or a pen plotter
when you're using an SVG. And if you're

in like desktop publishing or for
screen, your top left is up here and
then you're describing things as being
10 millimeters across or 120 pixels
down. And you're positioning things from
this top leftand corner. We're kind of
here's our page layout. This is where
we're going. So with our SVGs, this is
what we're going to be working with.
Conceptually, we're thinking about this
is the top leftand corner here. But if
we're doing G-code, which we might do,
and over here on one of these machines,
or you might have a machine that uses
G-code,
then 000 is our bottom leftand corner
here, and we're saying how far we want

to move up.
It's not a huge problem because we'll
just deal with that.
And as we work through all the different
